points.text: Instrument the endpoint with distributed tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to separate request‑processing time, DB time, and network latency.
Enable slow‑query logging on the database and capture query execution times for the affected endpoint.
Run continuous network monitoring (ping, traceroute, packet capture) to log latency spikes and packet loss rates.
Correlate timestamps of slow endpoint responses with DB slow‑query logs and network loss events to find overlap.
If DB time dominates, add missing indexes, rewrite inefficient queries, or increase connection pool size as a quick fix.
If network latency spikes dominate, check for congested links, adjust TCP retransmission settings, or route traffic via a faster path.
Temporarily enable caching (in‑memory or CDN) for the endpoint to reduce load while root cause is investigated.
Run a baseline benchmark with DB calls mocked out; if latency remains high, focus on network or application code.
Enable the DB slow‑query log and capture execution plans for the intermittent slow queries.
Run EXPLAIN on those queries to spot missing indexes or costly joins.
Measure query latency from a client on the DB host versus a remote client to separate network delay.
Use ping, traceroute or iPerf between app server and DB to detect packet loss and RTT spikes.
Check OS/network stats (netstat, TCP retransmits) for congestion during slow periods.
Quick fixes: add missing indexes, rewrite inefficient queries, increase connection pool size, enable caching.
If packet loss remains, consider moving the DB closer to the app server or using a more reliable network path.
Capture TCP dumps on the affected host; look for retransmissions, duplicate ACKs, or high RTT spikes.
Measure baseline network latency with ping or traceroute to the DB server to detect packet loss or routing issues.
Run a continuous DB query benchmark (e.g., sysbench) locally on the DB server to see if slow queries persist without network.
Check OS network queues (netstat -s, ss -s) for drops or errors that correlate with the slow periods.
Enable MySQL slow query log with a low threshold; compare timestamps of slow queries to network loss events.
Temporarily switch the client to a different network path or VPN; if performance improves, suspect the original path.
Quick fix: increase TCP retransmission timeout (sysctl net.ipv4.tcp_retries2) or enable TCP selective ACKs to mitigate loss.
